metric_value: $3 billion revenue; third-largest US PC distributor behind Ingram Micro and Tech Data
notes: IE at peak revenue; ranked #107 Fortune 500

metric_value: Speed of IT implementation was critical — IE needed to transform IT within 12-18 months to survive competitive pressure
notes: Kastner's first key finding: the distribution industry was consolidating rapidly

metric_value: IE positioned itself as leading-edge technology company — a reseller not just a distributor
notes: IE differentiated through value-added services vs. pure logistics

metric_value: IE lacked internal IT competency to execute a major systems transformation without external help
notes: Key factor: IE had no internal capacity to manage a project of IE/2000 scope

metric_value: IE's fundamental strategic error was pursuing a reseller/value-added model rather than low-cost distributor model as industry consolidated
notes: Kastner's opinion: IE misread industry direction toward commodity distribution

metric_value: Andersen Consulting performed competently on the IE/2000 engagement given the constraints imposed by IE
notes: Central opinion of the expert report

metric_value: IE owned the IE/2000 project — IE management made all key decisions including scope, timing, and technology choices
notes: Kastner concluded IE bore responsibility for project direction

metric_value: Andersen's IT Strategy Report was an excellent piece of work — thorough, well-researched, and appropriate for IE's situation
notes: Kastner reviewed the strategy document and found it professionally sound

metric_value: Option 2 (build new client-server system while maintaining legacy) was the best strategic choice among the three options presented
notes: Option 1: enhance MSD; Option 2: new build + maintain legacy; Option 3: package solution (SAP R/3)

metric_value: IE's long-term viability was questionable regardless of IT outcome — the PC distribution industry was consolidating to 2-3 major players
notes: Kastner predicted industry would consolidate; IE too small to survive

metric_value: IE filed for bankruptcy in 1996 and was liquidated by 1997
notes: Confirmed outcome; IE ceased operations entirely

metric_value: MicroAge filed for bankruptcy in 2000 — another mid-tier PC distributor eliminated by consolidation
notes: Validates Kastner's consolidation thesis

metric_value: Inacom filed for bankruptcy in 2001 — third mid-tier PC distributor eliminated
notes: Further validates industry consolidation prediction

metric_value: AS/400 was a viable platform for distribution in 1994 with strong RPG developer ecosystem and proven reliability
notes: Kastner assessed AS/400 as appropriate for IE's needs at the time

metric_value: AS/400 platform would remain viable for distribution applications well beyond IE's planning horizon
notes: Issue 7: Kastner disputed claim that AS/400 was obsolete

metric_value: IBM AS/400 (now IBM i) remains active and supported in 2025 — over 30 years after IE case; still widely used in distribution
notes: AS/400 longevity proved Kastner correct; platform thrived as IBM i

metric_value: HP 3000 was already in decline by 1994 — losing market share and developer support
notes: Referenced in Issues 5 and 6 regarding Andersen staffing

metric_value: HP announced end-of-life for HP 3000 in 2003; platform fully discontinued 2008
notes: Confirms HP 3000 declining trajectory noted in 1994

metric_value: MSD was IE's core distribution system — RPG-based on AS/400; functional but aging and difficult to extend
notes: IE's existing system that IE/2000 was meant to replace or augment

metric_value: MSD upgrade (Issue 1) was a reasonable interim step — keeping legacy operational while building new system was standard practice
notes: Kastner defended the MSD upgrade decision as prudent risk management

metric_value: Andersen's resignation from the IE/2000 project was justified given IE's failure to meet commitments and changing project conditions
notes: Issue 2: Kastner found Andersen had legitimate reasons to withdraw

metric_value: IE repeatedly changed project scope and requirements — a key factor in IE/2000 difficulties
notes: Issue 3: scope definition problems originated from IE management

metric_value: Andersen's work product quality was comparable to industry standards for major SI firms based on Aberdeen's research across 7 major SI firms
notes: Issue 4: Kastner benchmarked Andersen against peer firms

metric_value: Andersen's staffing levels and expertise were appropriate for the IE/2000 engagement scope
notes: Issue 5: staffing levels compared to industry norms for similar projects

metric_value: Andersen's Application Software Factory (ASF) methodology was a reasonable development approach — standard for large SI engagements of the era
notes: Issue 8: ASF was Andersen's proprietary SDLC methodology

metric_value: Client-server architecture was the correct strategic direction for IE — replacing terminal-based AS/400 applications with modern interfaces
notes: Foundation of Option 2 recommendation; mainstream industry direction

metric_value: Client-server became the dominant enterprise architecture by late 1990s before evolving to web-based and then cloud architectures
notes: Validates the technology direction recommended in 1994

metric_value: The Willbern Group's review of Andersen's work was limited in scope and methodology — a one-person firm without the depth to properly assess a major SI engagement
notes: Issue 10: Kastner critiqued the Willbern report as insufficiently rigorous

metric_value: IE revenue approximately $3 billion in 1993 — peak before decline
notes: IE's highest revenue year; ranked among top PC distributors

metric_value: SAP R/3 (Option 3) was evaluated but deemed too risky and expensive for IE — implementation timeline would exceed IE's survival window
notes: Option 3 rejected; SAP implementation typically 18-36 months in 1994

metric_value: SAP became the dominant enterprise ERP system globally; evolved from R/3 to ECC to S/4HANA
notes: SAP succeeded broadly but IE couldn't have waited for implementation

metric_value: Ingram Micro was the largest PC distributor — exemplified the low-cost high-volume model that was winning
notes: Ingram represented the industry direction IE failed to follow

metric_value: Ingram Micro survived as a leading distributor until acquired by Platinum Equity in 2021 for $7.2B — validating the full-line distributor model
notes: One of only two survivors from the 1994 top-tier distributors

metric_value: Tech Data merged with SYNNEX in 2021 to form TD SYNNEX — the other surviving major distributor
notes: Second survivor; confirmed Kastner's 2-3 player consolidation thesis

metric_value: Andersen Consulting rebranded as Accenture in January 2001 following arbitration with Arthur Andersen; became world's largest consulting firm
notes: Andersen thrived post-IE litigation; now $60B+ revenue

metric_value: PC distribution industry consolidating from 10+ players to 2-3 major full-line distributors; mid-tier players being squeezed by margin compression
notes: Central analytical framework of the report

metric_value: Kastner had 30+ years IT experience; EVP/CRO at Aberdeen since 1988; authored 150+ reports on IT strategy and outsourcing
notes: Appendix A: Kastner biography established credibility for opinions

metric_value: IE experienced significant management turnover during the IE/2000 project — destabilizing project governance and decision-making
notes: Multiple CEO/CIO changes during critical implementation period

metric_value: Enterprise systems integration in 1994 was inherently high-risk — industry failure rates for large IT transformations exceeded 50%
notes: Appendix research: Aberdeen QA data across 7 major SI firms showed high failure rates

metric_value: IE's IT spending was below industry averages for companies of its size — underinvestment contributed to competitive disadvantage
notes: IE had chronically underinvested in IT infrastructure

metric_value: RPG programming language had a mature but shrinking developer pool in 1994 — finding RPG talent was increasingly difficult
notes: Factor in AS/400 platform assessment; RPG developers aging out

metric_value: IE's market share was declining as Ingram Micro and Tech Data gained share through scale advantages and lower margins
notes: Revenue declining from $3B peak; competitive position eroding

metric_value: McKinsey had advised IE on strategic options — the consulting engagement preceded and informed the Andersen IT engagement
notes: McKinsey involvement established the strategic context for IE/2000

metric_value: Aberdeen's assessment drew on proprietary research including QA benchmarks across 7 major SI firms and extensive PC distribution industry analysis
notes: Appendices B-E contained supporting Aberdeen research

metric_value: Even with successful IT implementation IE would likely have been acquired or dissolved due to structural industry dynamics — IT was necessary but not sufficient for survival
notes: Kastner's most significant finding: IE's problems were strategic not technical
